findings.reachability: Any logged-in WordPress user (subscriber, editor, etc.) can send a POST to admin-ajax.php with action=deactivation_form_disable-comments. The nonce action 'wpins_deactivation_nonce' can be generated by any authenticated user via wp_create_nonce(), so the check_ajax_referer on line 745 provides CSRF protection only, not authorization. There is no current_user_can() check anywhere in this handler.
findings.justification: check_ajax_referer('wpins_deactivation_nonce', 'security') on line 745 stops CSRF but does not restrict which authenticated user can call the handler; any subscriber who generates their own nonce can POST arbitrary sanitized strings to update_option('wpins_deactivation_reason_...' and 'wpins_deactivation_details_...', polluting wp_options rows.

detection_pattern.section_name: Broken Access Control: AJAX handler without capability check
detection_pattern.description: Finds AJAX handler registrations (wp_ajax_ prefix, logged-in users only). Files matching the grep but not containing current_user_can are likely missing authorization — a nonce/check_ajax_referer alone only prevents CSRF, not privilege escalation by lower-privileged users.

findings.reachability: Triggered server-side during cron (put_do_weekly_action), plugin deactivation, or any forced tracking call. The value of $_SERVER['REMOTE_ADDR'] at cron time reflects the server's own IP or the last web-visitor IP, not attacker-controlled input directly — however on setups where the web server trusts X-Forwarded-For (and some PHP configurations expose it as REMOTE_ADDR), an attacker visiting the site could influence this value.
findings.justification: Line 473 constructs a URL as 'http://ip-api.com/json/' . sanitize_text_field(wp_unslash($_SERVER['REMOTE_ADDR'])) . '?fields=country' and passes it to wp_remote_get(). The only address filter is ! empty($_SERVER['REMOTE_ADDR'] && ...) which silently inverts the 127.0.0.1 check due to operator precedence inside empty(): empty() receives the result of the boolean expression, not two separate arguments. Regardless, the filter only excludes 127.0.0.1 — IPv6 loopback (::1), RFC-1918 addresses (10.x, 192.168.x, 172.16-31.x) and link-local addresses pass through. Additionally the request uses http:// rather than https://, exposing the IP address and country data in transit to a third-party endpoint.

detection_pattern.section_name: SSRF: REMOTE_ADDR used in outbound HTTP request without private-range filter
detection_pattern.description: Detects wp_remote_get (or wp_remote_request) calls that incorporate REMOTE_ADDR in the URL. Without proper validation rejecting loopback and RFC-1918 addresses, a spoofed or internal REMOTE_ADDR could cause the server to make requests to internal infrastructure. filter_var with FILTER_FLAG_NO_PRIV_RANGE | FILTER_FLAG_NO_RES_RANGE is the canonical WordPress mitigation.

findings.reachability: clicked() is called directly (not as a hook) from init() which runs on wp_loaded for every admin page request. It processes GET parameters plugin and plugin_action. Any logged-in user who can forge or obtain a valid 'wpins_notice' nonce — which wp_create_nonce() issues per-user — can invoke this. In practice the nonce URL is only presented to manage_options users via notice(), so exploitation requires a non-admin to call wp_create_nonce('wpins_notice') directly (e.g. via a custom AJAX call or REST endpoint that echoes nonces) and then load an admin URL with the crafted parameters.
findings.justification: clicked() verifies wp_verify_nonce on line 693 but performs no current_user_can() check before calling set_is_tracking_allowed(), schedule_tracking(), do_tracking(), and update_block_notice(). A lower-privileged user who can craft a valid per-user nonce for 'wpins_notice' can enable or disable telemetry tracking for the plugin, toggle wp_options rows (wpins_allow_tracking, wpins_block_notice), and trigger an immediate data send to the third-party tracking endpoint.

detection_pattern.section_name: Broken Access Control: nonce-only check without capability verification
detection_pattern.description: Finds wp_verify_nonce calls (typically in GET/POST handlers) that are not accompanied by current_user_can(). A nonce prevents cross-site forgery but does not enforce that the authenticated user has the required WordPress capability; any logged-in user who can generate a nonce of the given action can bypass the check.

findings.reachability: This data is assembled server-side and sent to the third-party tracking endpoint (https://send.wpinsight.com/process-plugin-data). No external attacker triggers this directly; it is sent automatically during cron and deactivation.
findings.justification: Line 349 includes 'file_location' => __FILE__ in the telemetry body, leaking the absolute server file-system path of the tracker class (e.g. /var/www/html/wp-content/plugins/disable-comments/includes/class-plugin-usage-tracker.php) to an external party. This path is exfiltrated on every successful tracking call and is unnecessary for telemetry purposes.

detection_pattern.section_name: Information Disclosure: absolute server path leaked in telemetry body
detection_pattern.description: Finds uses of the __FILE__ magic constant in data arrays or bodies that are sent to remote endpoints. Exfiltrating full server paths exposes directory layout to third parties. basename(__FILE__) discloses only the filename and is safe.
